risk_of_bias.support_text: Summary: The paper describes the groups as receiving saline (controls) or milrinone and calls the study an RCT, but it does not report how the allocation sequence was generated. There is no explicit mention of a random number table, computer-generated randomization, block/stratified randomization, central allocation, or any other random method. Comment: Because only the fact of randomization is implied by the study design and no sufficient details are given, the method of sequence generation cannot be judged as truly random.

Summary: The report describes the trial as an RCT and states that patients received either saline (controls) or milrinone, but it does not say how the assignment sequence was concealed from the person enrolling participants. There is no explicit mention of central allocation, sequentially numbered opaque sealed envelopes, sequentially numbered identical containers, or any other mechanism preventing foreknowledge of the next assignment. Comment: Because the paper reports random assignment but gives no allocation-concealment method, the risk of selection bias from foreknowledge remains unclear.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "All procedures were performed by the same cardiac surgeon and anesthesiologist blinded to the treatment groups." Comment: The report explicitly states that the personnel delivering the intervention were blinded. Because this is an intraoperative placebo-controlled RCT and the key outcomes are hemodynamic measurements and short-term clinical outcomes, lack of participant blinding is unlikely to introduce meaningful performance bias beyond the blinded surgical/anesthesia team.

Quote: "All procedures were performed by the same cardiac surgeon and anesthesiologist blinded to the treatment groups." Comment: The main outcomes were hemodynamic variables and right ventricular ejection fraction measured continuously by a computerized pulmonary artery catheter monitoring system and echocardiographic monitoring, plus short-term clinical outcomes recorded from postoperative follow-up. Because the key measurements were obtained by automated/standardized monitoring systems rather than subjective assessor judgement, and the clinicians involved were blinded to treatment groups, knowledge of allocation is unlikely to have influenced outcome measurement.

Quote: "N lost to follow‐up or withdrawn:0" and "N analysed:40"; also, the study planned for 20 patients in each group, 40 in total, and reports outcomes for both groups throughout follow-up. Comment: There was essentially complete follow-up with no reported withdrawals or missing outcome data, so attrition is very unlikely to have biased the results.
